title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a farmer called Claire talking about work-experience opportunities at an apple farm. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a farmer called Claire talking about work-experience opportunities at an apple farm. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A farmer called Claire talks about work-experience opportunities at an apple farm.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Claire, and my family runs Oakfield Apple Farm. Each autumn, we offer work-experience days to students interested in food production, business or outdoor work. The farm is on land which once belonged to a large private house. Our storage building was not always used for fruit: it started as a [9]horse stable[/9], and you can still see its original stone walls under the modern roof. We grow six varieties of apple, as well as a small number of pears.||People joining the experience are often surprised at how much preparation takes place before fruit is picked. On the first morning, students are not sent straight into the orchard. They take part in a short talk about [10]food quality[/10], learning why damaged fruit must be separated and how apples are stored without becoming bruised. Clothing should be practical. We supply picking bags and gloves, but everyone needs to bring [11]rubber boots[/11], because grass between the trees can stay wet for several hours after rain. There is a drying room for coats if the weather changes.||When picking begins, students work with a trained farm assistant. They may expect the reddest fruit to be the most suitable, but colour is not the only guide. Beginners first practise [12]sorting apples[/12], placing fruit into different boxes according to condition and intended use. The apples not attractive enough for sale in shops are not wasted. Most are taken to a nearby [13]juice factory[/13], where they are pressed into drinks sold locally. Fruit which cannot be used at all is added to compost for the orchards.||The farm welcomes visitors on Saturdays during harvest. Families can follow a marked route and watch demonstrations. This year's new visitor feature is a small [14]farm shop[/14], where people can buy apples, juice and products from nearby producers. Students on work experience may help there if they enjoy speaking to customers. In addition, we arrange one special learning afternoon. Last year a chef gave a talk on fruit desserts; this year students have shown more interest in [15]cider making[/15], so an expert will explain the process, although all tasting activities are strictly for adults.||Students who attend four complete days are given a [16]work reference[/16] outlining their punctuality and duties, which can be used in future applications. We also invite them to our end-of-harvest [17]bonfire evening[/17], with hot food and music for farm workers, volunteers and families. The farm is currently trying to reduce energy use in its cold storage building. Rather than buying new vehicles, we will use money raised at visitor weekends towards installing [18]solar panels[/18] on its roof. Application forms are available online, and we accept students from the age of sixteen.

dialogue.speaker: claire
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Claire, and my family runs Oakfield Apple Farm. Each autumn, we offer work-experience days to students interested in food production, business or outdoor work. The farm is on land which once belonged to a large private house. Our storage building was not always used for fruit: it started as a horse stable, and you can still see its original stone walls under the modern roof. We grow six varieties of apple, as well as a small number of pears.||People joining the experience are often surprised at how much preparation takes place before fruit is picked. On the first morning, students are not sent straight into the orchard. They take part in a short talk about food quality, learning why damaged fruit must be separated and how apples are stored without becoming bruised. Clothing should be practical. We supply picking bags and gloves, but everyone needs to bring rubber boots, because grass between the trees can stay wet for several hours after rain. There is a drying room for coats if the weather changes.||When picking begins, students work with a trained farm assistant. They may expect the reddest fruit to be the most suitable, but colour is not the only guide. Beginners first practise sorting apples, placing fruit into different boxes according to condition and intended use. The apples not attractive enough for sale in shops are not wasted. Most are taken to a nearby juice factory, where they are pressed into drinks sold locally. Fruit which cannot be used at all is added to compost for the orchards.||The farm welcomes visitors on Saturdays during harvest. Families can follow a marked route and watch demonstrations. This year's new visitor feature is a small farm shop, where people can buy apples, juice and products from nearby producers. Students on work experience may help there if they enjoy speaking to customers. In addition, we arrange one special learning afternoon. Last year a chef gave a talk on fruit desserts; this year students have shown more interest in cider making, so an expert will explain the process, although all tasting activities are strictly for adults.||Students who attend four complete days are given a work reference outlining their punctuality and duties, which can be used in future applications. We also invite them to our end-of-harvest bonfire evening, with hot food and music for farm workers, volunteers and families. The farm is currently trying to reduce energy use in its cold storage building. Rather than buying new vehicles, we will use money raised at visitor weekends towards installing solar panels on its roof. Application forms are available online, and we accept students from the age of sixteen.

questions.question: The farm's fruit storage building was originally a (9) .
questions.explanation: Claire explains that the building started as a 'horse stable'.

questions.explanation: Claire states that the first talk covers 'food quality'.

questions.explanation: Claire asks all students to bring 'rubber boots'.

questions.explanation: Claire says beginners first practise 'sorting apples'.

questions.explanation: Claire says most imperfect fruit goes to a 'juice factory'.

questions.explanation: Claire identifies the 'farm shop' as a new feature.
